---
title: "Introduction to Generative AI and LLMs"
subtitle: "Day 1 -- Lecture A"
pagetitle: "d1: Gen AI and LLM"
author: Menuka Bhandari
date: 2025-07-04
institute: "CFAES Bioinformatics Core, OSU"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-color: "silver"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme_mod.scss]
    css: ../slides.css
    smaller: true
    scrollable: false
    progress: true
    incremental: false
    slide-number: true
    slide-level: 2
    highlight-style: pygments
    embed-resources: false # Can't be used with chalkboard
    chalkboard:
      theme: whiteboard
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 1
    toc-title: TBD
    footer: "[~ Gen and agentic AI workshop ~](https://cfaes-bioinfo.github.io/agentic-ai-workshop/)"
    logo: ../img/CFAES-Horiz-RGBHEX.svg
    menu:
      width: wide
      numbers: true
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
bibliography: refs.bib
---

## Overview

- Introduction to Artificial intelligence (AI) and Large language model (LLM)

- Transformer architectures - Encoder only model, Decoder only model, Encoder-decoder model 

- Reinforcement Learning 

- Tokens, Context windows, Embedding 

- Chat-and-answer vs. agentic AI 

- Different models with different capabilities and costs 

## Introduction to AI

- **Artificial intelligence (AI)**\
  - AI is the branch of computer science that **simulates human intelligence and trains computers to learn human behaviors**
  - Objective - make machines think like humans and learn human behaviors such as reasoning, planning, predicting, problem solving, decision making 
  - AI is a multidisciplinary technology with an ability to integrate cognition, machine learning, emotion recognition, human computer interaction, data storage and decision making
  - Applications of AI in biology - protein sequence & structure prediction, recognize the functional element of DNA, drug discovery etc

## Introduction to AI(cont.)

- **Artificial intelligence (AI)**\

![**Figure 1.** Branches of Artificial Intelligence: LLM falls under machine learning, neural network, NLP](/img/branches_of_AI.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

## Introduction to LLM

- **Language model (LM)** \
  - LM is a statistical or machine learning model that learns the patterns and relationship within sequences
  - It estimates the probability of a sequence and predicts the next word or token based on the surrounding context.
  - Example; Consider the two sentences:
    - The cat sat on the mat
    - The cat sat on the refrigerator
  - The first sentence occurs more frequently in language, thus LM assigns higher probability to it
  - Traditional LM (e.g., N-gram models) struggle with rare or unseen words/tokens & capturing long-range dependencies
  
## Introduction to LLM (cont.)

- **Large language model (LLM)**\
  - LLMs are advanced LM that are core of many AI applications
  - LLMs are built on **Transformer architecture** & trained on **massive collections of data**
  - **Large** in LLM refers to both the enormous number of model parameters and the vast quantity of training data used during development 
  - LLMs are trained to predict the **next token or sequence of token** in a piece of text
  - Two key characteristics of LLMS are **large parameter counts** and **in-context learning capabilities**
  - Examples; ChatGPT (Open AI), Claude (Anthropic), Gemini (Google Deep Mind), Llama (Meta)
  
## Introduction to LLM (cont.)

- **What can LLM do for the biological scientists?**\
  - Generate code from the natural language description, auto complete code, execute complex tasks, manage multiple files and execute everything in a secure environment
  - Agentic AI can execute scripts, troubleshoot errors, validate outputs, and propose changes autonomously
  - Programming is no longer a barrier to biological scientists 

![**Figure 2.** Evolution of LLM](/img/evolution_LLM.png){fig-align="center" width="30%"}

## Introduction to LLM (cont.)

- **Transformer**\
  - **Foundation of modern LLM** introduced in 2017
   - Can process an entire input sequence in parallel - faster and efficient computation
  - Ability to capture long-range dependencies within text - understand relationships between words and phrases across extensive contexts
   - Relies on a **self-attention mechanism** - enables model to analyze and weigh the importance of input sequence while making predictions

**Advantages of Transformer**\
•	Read the whole sequence at once \
•	Capture long-range relationships \
•	Train much faster

**Three Transformer architectures** - Encoder-only model, Decoder-only model & Encoder-Decoder model

## Transformer architectures

- **Encoder-only model (Google’s BERT) **\
  - **Understands the input data**
  - Process the input data and convert it to the contextualized internal representation (**embedding**)
  - Data is processed in bidirectional manner - understand context left and right side
  - **Self attention** - each token in a sequence can look at every other token in the same sequence to understand its context
  - Example; **DNABERT**

## Transformer architectures (cont.)
- **Encoder-only model (Google’s BERT) **\

  - DNABERT is a AI model that uses DNA as a language
  - Tokens are generated based on k-mers (DNABERT) or Byte Pair Encoding (DNABERT-2)
  - DNABERT reads the entire DNA sequence and understand the biological function of even distant nucleotide bases
  - DNABERT can recognize important functional regions such as promoters, splice sites, and transcription factor binding sites \
  
  **Read → Understand → Predict**
  
## Transformer architectures (cont.)
- **Decoder-only model (OpenAI GPT series)**\
  - Decoder models are designed to generate the output sequence/new sequence
  - Predicts the next token based on the context of all previously generated tokens 
  - Unidirectional - process sequence from left to right to predict what comes next
  - Example - **ProGen**
  - ProGen - protein language model trained on millions of raw protein sequences
  - ProGen predicts the next amino acid based on the amino acids that come before it generating entirely new protein sequences from de novo \
  
![](/img/encoder_decoder_model.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"}
  
## Transformer architectures (cont.)
- **Encoder-decoder model**\
  - Combines both understanding (encoding) and generation (decoding)
  - Understands the input and generates the output
  - Example: **Protein structure-sequence T5 (Prott5)**
  - ProstT5 is a protein language model based on T5 encoder-decoder architecture - translate protein sequence and structure
  - The encoder learns a contextual representation of the input (sequence or structure), & decoder generates the corresponding output (structure or sequence)
  - This enables bidirectional translation between protein sequence and structural information \
     **Read → Understand → Translate**


## Reinforcement learning (RL)

  - A machine learning approach where an agent learns by trial and error
  - Agent interacts with an environment to maximize rewards and minimize penalties
  - Successful actions are reinforced, while poor actions are discouraged
  -  Agent improves its decision-making through experience overtime
  - Advantage: Agent can learn in an unknown environment
  - Limitations: long training times, low sample efficiency and stability concerns
  - Example; De novo Drug discovery to optimize drug candidates \
  - In protein engineering, an AI model generates protein sequences, receives rewards for desirable properties (e.g., higher stability or stronger binding affinity), and learns to generate improved proteins over time
  
## Chat-and-answer vs. agentic AI

![](/img/chat_agentic_AI.png){fig-align="center" width="10%"}

## Different models with different capabilities and costs
